title: Chapter Forty: Bly
content: The drive is shorter than I remember.

Six months ago, the road from the village to Bly Manor felt like a passage through layers—each turn peeling back another skin of the world until I arrived somewhere that wasn't quite real. Now it's just a road. Two lanes. Hedgerows. The occasional sheep looking up with the vacant curiosity of creatures who have never once worried about narrative.

I park in the new lot—gravel, modest, spaces marked with plain white lines—and sit in the car for a long time. The engine ticks as it cools. My hands stay on the wheel, ten and two, the way my mother taught me before she taught me anything else worth keeping.
I haven't been back since the trial.

The prosecution didn't need me to return. They had the documents, the testimony, the paper trail that Peter Quint left behind like breadcrumbs leading to the gingerbread house—except in this version, the house was real and the witch was a man in a tweed jacket who believed that authority was indistinguishable from divinity. I gave my statement from a room in London with bad lighting and a glass of water I never touched. I answered their questions. I went home.
Home. That word got complicated somewhere along the way.

But the trust—the public trust that seized Bly after Henry's conviction, the board of survivors and advocates and one remarkably persistent journalist who wouldn't let the story die—they invited me back. Not to speak. Not to consult. Just to see. "The building has changed," their letter said. "We thought you might want to know that it's possible."
I didn't want to know. That's a lie. I was terrified to know. I came anyway.
The house is smaller.

That's the first thing I notice as I get out of the car and walk toward the drive. Bly Manor, which once loomed in my memory like a cathedral built by angry architects, is just... a house. A large house, certainly. Old stone, mullioned windows, the geometry of something that has stood for centuries and will likely stand for centuries more. But the proportions are ordinary now. The gables don't lean toward me like interrogators. The windows don't watch.
I grew. That's what happened. I grew, and the house stayed the same size, and so the house shrank in the only way that mattered—the way things shrink when you stop being afraid of them.

The cottagecore aesthetic is gone. I notice it immediately, the absence of it, the way you notice silence after music has been playing for so long you forgot it was there. No artfully draped linen. No curated clusters of wildflowers in mismatched pitchers. No soft focus, no warm filters, no performance of gentleness designed to disguise the machinery of control. The front garden has been replanted with things that don't need to be picturesque—hardy shrubs, practical rows of herbs, a vegetable patch that looks like someone actually eats from it rather than photographing it.
The door is navy blue. Not the faded grey of before, not the Instagram-ready sage that the Bly account used to feature. Just navy. Solid. A color that doesn't ask to be liked.
I press the buzzer. It makes a sensible sound—no chimes, no echo, just a firm note that says *someone will come*.
The woman who answers is not Mrs. Grose.

She's younger, mid-thirties maybe, with the kind of face that has learned to be calm without being distant. She wears a lanyard that says *Jess — Recovery Support* and a jumper that has a small paint stain on the cuff. She looks at me the way people look at me now—with the careful recognition of someone who has seen my face in articles and is deciding whether to acknowledge it.
"Maeve Bly?" she says.
"Yes."
"We're glad you came. Come in."

I step over the threshold. The hallway smells of fresh paint—eggshell white, clean and bright, covering whatever lived in the wallpaper before. The antique rugs are gone, replaced by functional carpet in a color I can only describe as *safe*. It's neutral without being cold. It's the color of a place that expects people to walk on it, to spill things, to be human without apology. Under my shoes, it gives slightly, and the give feels deliberate—like the house is learning to be gentle.

The east wing is therapy rooms now. Jess points them out as we walk—closed doors with small signs, *Sunflower Room*, *Oak Room*, names that are earnest and unpretentious and trying so hard to be kind that I almost smile. Through one slightly ajar door, I hear the low murmur of two voices, neither of them urgent, and the soft percussion of someone stirring tea.
"It's not what it was," Jess says, and I can't tell if she's apologizing or observing.
"Good," I say.

She leaves me then, with directions to explore at my own pace. "Take your time," she says. "This is your visit." And then she's gone, and I'm standing in the hallway of Bly Manor alone, listening to the sounds of a building that has been repurposed for healing instead of harm.
The staircase is the same. I climb it anyway.
I find Mrs. Grose in the former servants' quarters.

The space has been converted into small flats—efficient, private, each with its own door and its own number. Hers is 3. The door is painted the same navy as the entrance, and there's a small plaque beside it that reads *Grose* in letters that are matter-of-fact. No flourish. No hierarchy.
I knock.

She opens the door and for a moment we just look at each other. She's thinner than she was. Not frail—stripped. Like something that has been boiled down to its essential elements. Her hair is shorter. Her eyes are the same, though. Those dark, knowing eyes that saw everything at Bly and carried it like stones in her pockets for decades.
"Maeve."
"Mrs. Grose."
"Come in, then. Sit down."

The flat is small—a sitting area, a kitchenette, a door that must lead to a bedroom. There are photographs on the wall. I don't look at them closely. Some things aren't mine to see. There's a teapot on the counter, and she moves toward it with the automatic efficiency of someone who has spent a lifetime pouring tea for other people.
"Are you still doing that?" I ask. "Making tea for everyone who walks through the door?"
"Old habits." She sets a cup in front of me. "But now I make it for myself too. That's the difference."

I wrap my hands around the cup. It's warm. The warmth travels up through my palms and into my wrists, and I realize how cold I've been—not today, not from the weather, but for months. The cold of keeping yourself braced against the next impact. I take a sip. It's the best tea I've ever had, and I don't know if that's because of the tea or because I'm finally letting myself taste something.
"You live here now," I say.
"I'm a client." She says it without shame. "Residential recovery. Same program as everyone else. Different circumstances, same—" she pauses, searching for the word—"same damage."
"Did they ask you to stay?"

"No one asked. I chose to stay." She sits across from me, and her hands are still—steadier than I remember them. "This is where I failed. This is where I'll make it right. Even if it takes the rest of my life."
"It wasn't your failure."

"Wasn't it?" She looks at me, and there's no accusation in it, no self-pity—just the honest reckoning of a woman who spent years believing that loyalty meant silence. "I saw the signs. I saw the children. I saw the way he looked at them, the way he arranged things, the way the house became a mechanism for—" She stops. Breathes. "I told myself I was protecting them by staying. By keeping things running. By being there. But I was protecting him. I was protecting the system that let him operate. And I called it duty."
The words hang in the air between us. I don't try to soften them. She wouldn't want me to.
"The program here," I say. "Is it helping?"

"Slowly." She takes a sip of her own tea. "The counselor says I have to stop thinking of myself as the person who failed and start thinking of myself as the person who's still here. It sounds simple. It isn't."
"No," I say. "It isn't."
She looks at me—really looks, the way she used to look at me across the kitchen table when I was still performing confidence like a parlour trick.
"You're different," she says.
"I know."
"Quieter."
"The noise was mostly fear," I say. "I didn't realize how loud I was until the fear stopped."
"Has it stopped?"

I consider this. The honest answer is no—not entirely. Fear doesn't stop. It just changes address. But the fear I carry now is different. It's the fear of ordinary things, of getting things wrong, of starting over. It's not the fear of a house that watched me or a man who could unmake me with a word. It's smaller. More manageable. The kind of fear you can build a life around instead of the kind that demolishes one.
"Enough," I say. "Enough for now."
She nods. She understands. Of course she does.
The tower is a memorial library.
The sign on the door says so in clean, sans-serif letters: *The Rebecca Jessel Memorial Library*. Below it, smaller: *A warning, not a shrine.*
I push the door open.

The room is unrecognizable. Peter's desk—that monolithic slab of dark wood where he sat and wrote and planned and controlled—is gone. In its place: a reading table. Round. Light wood. Encircled by simple chairs. Designed for conversation, not authority.

His books are still here. That surprised me, the first time I heard about it. The trust debated what to do with Peter's library—the extensive collection on behavioral psychology, on institutional dynamics, on the architecture of compliance. They could have burned them. They could have locked them away. Instead, they kept them. But they changed them.

I pull a volume from the shelf. *Institutional Dynamics and the Construction of Trust*, Peter's own copy, heavily underlined in his small, precise hand. But there are other marks now—annotations in different pens, different handwritings, the voices of survivors who have read these pages and answered back.
In the margin beside a passage about the mechanisms of control, someone has written: *You can't control what happened to you. You can only choose what happens next.*

I run my finger over the words. The ink is slightly raised—recent, written with a pen that pressed hard, like the person needed the words to be permanent, to be physical, to exist in the world rather than just in the mind.

I turn more pages. More annotations. Some are brief—*This happened to me*, or *He said this to me too*, or simply *I survived*. Others are longer, dense paragraphs that argue with the text, that dismantle its assumptions, that refuse the frameworks Peter built. The margin has become a conversation. The book is no longer a monologue delivered from a position of power. It's a dialogue, and the people who were silenced are speaking now.
I put the book back. My hands are shaking, but only slightly.
The tower has been reclaimed.

I can feel it—the absence of that weight. When I stood in this room before, the air was thick with Peter's presence, his certainty, his absolute conviction that the world operated according to his design. Now the air is just air. The views from the windows are still the same—grounds, sky, the distant glitter of the lake—but the windows aren't watching. They're just letting in the light.
I stand there for a long time. I breathe. The room smells of paper and wood polish and the faint, clean ghost of new paint. It smells like a place where people work and think and argue and heal.
It smells nothing like a Tower.
The path to the lake is narrower than I remember. Or maybe I'm just walking slower.

The grass is longer now—less manicured, allowed to grow into something that resembles a meadow rather than a lawn. Wildflowers have colonized the edges of the path, and I can hear insects in them, the small, purposeful sounds of creatures who have work to do. A bumblebee passes my shoulder on a trajectory only it understands.
The lake is there.
The bench is there.

I sit down. The wood is cold through my trousers—a sharp, specific kind of cold that has nothing to do with metaphor. Just wood, just November, just the way things feel when you let yourself feel them.
The plaque is brass, tarnished already from the weather. The words are etched into it:
*In memory of Rebecca Jessel. She found the truth. It should not have cost her everything.*
I read it twice. Three times. The words don't change. They won't. That's the point.

Rebecca Jessel. I didn't know her. I know her now—through the files, through the testimony, through the shape she left in the world, like a hand pressed into wet concrete. She came to Bly as a teacher. She found what Peter had built. She tried to stop it. And she—

No. That's not the whole story. That's the story the trial told, the one that fits into the arc of victim and villain and justice delayed. The full story is larger and messier and full of the contradictions of a real person who was more than the worst thing that happened to her. I didn't know her. I can't eulogize her. All I can do is sit on this bench and acknowledge the cost, and try to live in a way that doesn't add to it.

The surface of the lake is grey. Not silver—grey. The color of water under a November sky. No impossible reflections. No faces staring up from the depths. No doubling, no haunting, no trick of light that makes the world seem like a story with yourself at the center.
Just water.
Just sky.
Just the quiet of a place that has stopped being a narrative and started being a home.

I close my eyes. I listen. I hear wind in the reeds at the lake's edge. I hear a bird—something small and uninterested in me—calling from somewhere in the trees. I hear the faint creak of the bench as my weight settles deeper into the wood. I hear my own breathing, slower than it's been in months, deeper, the breathing of someone who has finally stopped running and is discovering that stillness isn't the same as stopping.
I am still here.

That's not a small thing. That's not a concession. That's the whole fact of it, the bedrock everything else is built on. I walked into Bly Manor looking for a story, and I found one—not the kind I wanted, not the kind that can be told in a single satisfying arc, but the kind that goes on after the telling stops, the kind that requires living rather than narrating.

The cracks are still there. They always will be. But I'm starting to understand that cracks aren't damage—they're architecture. They're the places where the light comes through. They're the lines that prove the thing survived.
I am still here.
I open my eyes.

The lake is still. The sky is wide. The bench is cold and solid beneath me, and my hands are resting on my thighs, and I am not reaching for my phone to document this moment, not composing a caption in my head, not thinking about how this looks from the outside.
I take out my phone anyway.

Not to post. Not to record. Not to bear witness in the way I've been bearing witness for months now—the urgent, compulsive documentation of a woman afraid that if she stops writing, the story will swallow her whole.

I open my contacts. I scroll. I find the number I saved three weeks ago, the one I've been staring at late at night when the apartment is quiet and the future feels like a room I'm not sure I'm allowed to enter.
I dial.
It rings twice.
"Hello? Admissions office."
My voice, when it comes, is steady. Not brave—steady. There's a difference.
"Yes, this is Maeve Bly. I'm calling about the psychology program. I'd like to enroll."
There's a pause. The sound of keyboard strokes. "Maeve Bly—can you spell that?"
I spell it. B-L-Y. The name I've carried my whole life, the name that led me here, the name that means *the place where it happened* and also *the person who walked out the other side.*
"Thank you. Let me pull up your file . . ."
I sit on the bench by the lake at Bly Manor, and I wait. The water doesn't move. The wind doesn't signify. The world doesn't hold its breath.
I'm going back to school. Not as an investigator chasing ghosts through the architecture of old houses. Not as a player in someone else's game. Not as a narrator trying to sculpt meaning from chaos.
As a student.
As someone who doesn't know yet and is ready to learn.

For the first time in my life, I'm choosing what to become instead of being shaped by what happened to me. It doesn't feel dramatic. It doesn't feel like an ending or a beginning. It feels like exhaling—like releasing breath I didn't know I was holding, like letting go of tension I'd mistaken for structure, like the simple, terrifying, ordinary act of being alive and deciding what comes next.
"Yes, I'm here," I say. "I'm ready."
